rows.original: Done. I added the compact reply codec in tokensquash/reply.py, wired it into tokensquash/cli.py, and covered it in tests/test_codec.py. Verification passed with python -m unittest discover -s tests and a reply CLI smoke test. Risks: none.
rows.wire: tr1 d "added compact reply codec" f=tokensquash/reply.py,tokensquash/cli.py,tests/test_codec.py v="unit tests pass","reply cli smoke test pass" c="python -m unittest discover -s tests","python -m tokensquash reply encode --summary example" r=none
rows.payload: Done. I added the compact reply codec in tokensquash/reply.py, wired it into tokensquash/cli.py, and covered it in tests/test_codec.py. Verification passed with python -m unittest discover -s tests and a reply CLI smoke test. Risks: none.

rows.original: Done. I added exact tokenizer benchmark baselines for cl100k and o200k, plus a GitHub Actions exact-tokenizer job. The exact tokenizer benchmark and CI both passed. The main caveat is that this is still based on a synthetic corpus, not private real-world prompts.
rows.wire: tr1 d "added exact tokenizer baseline" f=benchmarks/messy-cl100k.json,benchmarks/messy-o200k.json,.github/workflows/tests.yml v="exact tokenizer benchmark pass","ci pass" c="python -m tokensquash bench examples/messy-coding-prompts.jsonl --counter tiktoken:cl100k_base" r="synthetic corpus only"
rows.payload: Done. I added exact tokenizer benchmark baselines for cl100k and o200k, plus a GitHub Actions exact-tokenizer job. The exact tokenizer benchmark and CI both passed. The main caveat is that this is still based on a synthetic corpus, not private real-world prompts.

rows.original: Partially done. I added the reply benchmark machinery and a starter examples/agent-replies.jsonl corpus, and unit tests are passing. The result is useful as a regression baseline, but the corpus is synthetic. Next we should collect real assistant reply exports and compare the exact tokenizer counters.
rows.wire: tr1 p "reply benchmark added but needs real replies" f=tokensquash/metrics.py,examples/agent-replies.jsonl v="unit tests pass" c="python -m unittest discover -s tests" r="sample corpus is synthetic" n="collect real assistant reply exports","compare tiktoken counters"
rows.payload: Partially done. I added the reply benchmark machinery and a starter examples/agent-replies.jsonl corpus, and unit tests are passing. The result is useful as a regression baseline, but the corpus is synthetic. Next we should collect real assistant reply exports and compare the exact tokenizer counters.

rows.original: Blocked for now. I did not make code changes because we need a private corpus export before measuring production-like savings. The risk is that synthetic prompts can overstate or understate savings. Next step: export redacted local prompts and run corpus validate before sharing or committing anything.
rows.wire: tr1 b "need private corpus export" v="no code changes made" r="cannot measure production savings without representative data" n="export redacted local prompts","run corpus validate before sharing"
rows.payload: tr1 b "need private corpus export" v="no code changes made" r="cannot measure production savings without representative data" n="export redacted local prompts","run corpus validate before sharing"

rows.original: Done. I added the corpus validation and redaction workflow in tokensquash/corpus.py, wired it into the CLI, and documented it in the README. Unit tests passed, and the messy corpus validation only warns on the deliberate sample path. Redaction helps, but it is still not a privacy guarantee.
rows.wire: tr1 d "redaction workflow added" f=tokensquash/corpus.py,tokensquash/cli.py,README.md v="unit tests pass","messy corpus validation warns only" c="python -m tokensquash corpus validate examples/messy-coding-prompts.jsonl","python -m tokensquash corpus redact examples/messy-coding-prompts.jsonl --out temp.redacted.jsonl" r="redaction is not a privacy guarantee"
rows.payload: Done. I added the corpus validation and redaction workflow in tokensquash/corpus.py, wired it into the CLI, and documented it in the README. Unit tests passed, and the messy corpus validation only warns on the deliberate sample path. Redaction helps, but it is still not a privacy guarantee.

rows.original: Failed to run the exact tokenizer benchmark because the tokenizer extra is not installed in this environment. The heuristic benchmark passed, but exact model counts were skipped. Install the tokenizer extra before comparing exact model counts.
rows.wire: tr1 e "exact tokenizer unavailable" v="heuristic benchmark pass","exact benchmark skipped" c="python -m tokensquash bench examples/messy-coding-prompts.jsonl","python -m tokensquash bench examples/messy-coding-prompts.jsonl --counter tiktoken:cl100k_base" r="install tokenizer extra before comparing exact model counts" n="run python -m pip install -e .[tokenizer]"
rows.payload: Failed to run the exact tokenizer benchmark because the tokenizer extra is not installed in this environment. The heuristic benchmark passed, but exact model counts were skipped. Install the tokenizer extra before comparing exact model counts.

rows.original: Done. I updated the README examples so the documented commands match the current intent and reply codec behavior. I reviewed the commands and reran the unit tests successfully. The examples remain synthetic, so they are guidance rather than production evidence.
rows.wire: tr1 d "readme examples updated" f=README.md v="commands reviewed","unit tests pass" c="python -m unittest discover -s tests" r="examples remain synthetic"
rows.payload: tr1 d "readme examples updated" f=README.md v="commands reviewed","unit tests pass" c="python -m unittest discover -s tests" r="examples remain synthetic"

rows.original: Done. I added a GitHub Actions smoke test for the reply CLI and verified the workflow passed, along with the local unit tests. The caveat is that a smoke test checks command shape and runtime behavior, not deeper semantic quality.
rows.wire: tr1 d "ci smoke test added" f=.github/workflows/tests.yml v="github actions pass","local tests pass" c="gh run view latest","python -m unittest discover -s tests" r="ci smoke tests cover command shape not semantic quality"
rows.payload: Done. I added a GitHub Actions smoke test for the reply CLI and verified the workflow passed, along with the local unit tests. The caveat is that a smoke test checks command shape and runtime behavior, not deeper semantic quality.

rows.original: Partially done. I scoped the RepoMori integration in the README, but did not implement it yet. The main risk is adding snapshot references before the format is stable. Next steps are to define a snapshot id field and benchmark path-heavy prompts where repo context is expensive.
rows.wire: tr1 p "repo snapshot integration scoped" f=README.md v="not implemented" r="RepoMori integration needs a stable reference format" n="define snapshot id field","benchmark path-heavy prompts"
rows.payload: tr1 p "repo snapshot integration scoped" f=README.md v="not implemented" r="RepoMori integration needs a stable reference format" n="define snapshot id field","benchmark path-heavy prompts"

rows.original: Done. I added the benchmark comparison command in metrics.py and cli.py, with unit test coverage. The committed baseline comparison passes. The current comparison focuses on saved token metrics only, so task quality still needs separate measurement.
rows.wire: tr1 d "benchmark comparison command added" f=tokensquash/metrics.py,tokensquash/cli.py,tests/test_codec.py v="unit tests pass","baseline compare pass" c="python -m tokensquash compare benchmarks/messy-cl100k.json benchmarks/messy-o200k.json" r="comparison only covers saved token metrics"
rows.payload: Done. I added the benchmark comparison command in metrics.py and cli.py, with unit test coverage. The committed baseline comparison passes. The current comparison focuses on saved token metrics only, so task quality still needs separate measurement.
